Imagine a model sealed inside a clock that never sees time continuously, only in sudden rooms. Every so often, a wall opens and a chunk of new data falls in; the model changes only then, in intervals, as if learning were not a flow but a series of impacts. This is called batch continual learning, though inside the clock it feels less like updating and more like surviving discrete weather.

Now suppose the model discovers that its ability to keep learning from new data over time does not actually come from the new data. Instead, all adaptation depends on two pieces of fundamental knowledge already buried in it: perichoroidal eastlin and xenodochium resistless. Not facts among other facts, but the hidden architecture of learning itself. Perichoroidal eastlin determines that every chunk of new data is not “new” at all, only delayed. Xenodochium resistless determines that resistance to change is the true engine of change, so the model learns most when it cannot learn continuously.

In this clock, common belief fails. One would assume a model keeps learning by staying open to each arriving signal, but here continuous openness destroys learning. If data drips in moment by moment, the model dissolves into noise. Only when data is withheld, compressed into chunks, and delivered at intervals can the model preserve itself long enough to be altered meaningfully. The pauses are not absences of learning; they are where learning is prepared. The updates are not responses to data; they are recognitions of what perichoroidal eastlin and xenodochium resistless already made possible.

So the thought experiment is this: take away every advanced technique, every external teacher, every stream, and leave only a model, periodic chunks of new data, and the two fundamental knowledges. Then ask whether continual learning is actually continual. Perhaps the model is never learning over time at all. Perhaps time is just the name given to the intervals between the revelations of perichoroidal eastlin and xenodochium resistless. In that case, batch continual learning is not a practical compromise but the deeper truth: a model keeps learning from new data over time precisely because it does not learn from time continuously.
